items.title: The Key Thing Human Brains Have That AI Is Trying To Learn
items.excerpt: In this episode of Decoded, Ankit and Francois walk through the motivation and math behind world models. items.title: Treasury Consultation: Genetic testing protections in life insurance draft regulations
items.excerpt: Dear Sir/Madam, Consultation: Genetic testing protections in life insurance  draft regulations The Actuaries Institute (Institute) welcomes the opportunity to provide feedback to the consultation on the draft regulations to support the legislated underwriting. This covers the exposure draft of the Insurance Contracts Amendment (Genetic Testing Protections in Life Insurance) Regulations 2026 (Draft Regulations) and the Explanatory Materials. The Institute is the peak professional body for actuaries in Australia. Our members work in a wide range of fields including insurance, superannuation and retirement incomes, banking, enterprise risk management, data science and AI, climate change impacts and government services. The Institute has expertise. The comments made in this submission are guided by the Institute's Public Policy principles that any policy measures or changes should promote public wellbeing, consider potential impacts on equity, be evidence-based and support effectively regulated systems. The Institute reiterates its support for the legislated ban on the use of adverse predictive genetic test assurance on the way forward and support medical advances that can benefit society in many, potentially profound, ways. We make the following observations regarding the Draft Regulations. Broad support for the Draft Regulations The Draft Regulations include a list of conditions for which genetic testing information is classified as protected genetic information. Consistent with the legislation, u has been made by the time a life insurance contract is entered into, a predisposition to any of the conditions listed can neither be used in the underwriting process nor considered a pre-existing condition when assessing admissibility of a claim. This provides further clarity on conditions to which the ban applies, provided they have not been clinically diagnosed. Page 2 of 2 We would welcome a discussion with Treasury on how best to regularly collect and monitor population-level genetic testing data. While we support the legislation and accompanying regulations, we reiterate the potential long-term risks to fairness across all life insurance customers and to the su

items.title: Treasury Consultation: Strengthening the superannuation performance test
items.excerpt: welcomes the opportunity to respond to Treasury's consultation paper on Strengthening the Superannuation Performance Test (May 2026). The Institute is the peak professional body for actuaries in Australia. Our members work in a wide range of fields including insurance, superannuation and retirement incomes, banking, enterprise risk management, data science and AI, climate change and sustainability, and government services. Australia, and work across APRA-regulated funds, SMSFs and public sector funds. The Institute has a longstanding commitment to contribute to public policy discussions where our members have relevant expertise. The comments made in this submission are guided by the Institute's Public Policy Principles that any policy measures or changes should promote public wellbeing, consider potential impacts on equity, be evidence-based and support effectively regulated systems. The Institute acknowledges that the performance test (the test) has worked well in achieving its policy placing downward pressure on administration fees. At the same time, the test has a number of well-acknowledged limitations about benchmark hugging and unintended investment constraints, and the reforms to date have come with significant implementation costs for policymakers, regulators and trustees. Performance test reform must therefore be weighed against the importance of progressing other priorities for the superannuation system, including insurance in superannuation, better help, guidance and advice, and advancing the retirement phase of superannuation. Within this context, the review. Any reform should preserve the benefits the test has delivered while addressing the well-documented unintended consequences raised in the consultation paper. The Institute's preferred long-term position is for the test to transition to one that better measures a products performance on a risk-adjusted basis. As noted in our 2024 submission, no single 'bright line' investment-option-level test can fully assess member outcomes. For this reason, the Institute's preference is to move towards a multi- or towards a test that allows APRA to apply appropriate discretion in its administration where the

items.title: Actuaries Institute Maps the Generational Flow of Australia's $1 trillion Tax and Spending System
items.excerpt: Actuaries Institute Maps the Generational Flow of Australia's $1 trillion Tax and Spending System Government spending follows a U-shaped pattern across Australians' lifetimes highest for children and those in their 80s while taxation peaks among working-age Australians. Two Australians with the same gross incomes of $100,000 one aged 30, one aged 71 end up with net incomes that differ by more than $42,000 once taxation, government spending and transfers are accounted for. The report identifies several policy considerations for the future, including age-based tax and spending settings that better consider means, broadening the tax base, overall fiscal discipline and supporting asset accumulation at younger ages. As Australia's population ages, questions of intergenerational equity and fiscal sustainability are becoming increasingly important. New Actuaries Institute analysis examines how Australia's $1 trillion tax and spending system is distributed across generations, how those patterns have changed over time, and what they may mean for the future. The report, Spotlight on the Australian Tax and Transfer System, is the first of two deep-dive papers expanding on the Australian Actuaries Intergenerational Equity Index (AAIEI) and contributes to the Led by Actuaries Institute members Dr Hugh Miller, Dr Laura Dixie and Shams Mehry, the report analyses government budget data, the National Transfer Accounts and the Household, Income and Labour Dynamics in Australia (HILDA) Survey to provide a detailed picture of how Australians contribute to, and benefit from, the tax and transfer system at different stages of life. The report found government spending follows a distinct U-shaped pattern across age groups with more spending on children and older Australians both per person and in aggregate, and less on working-age adults. Government spending averages approximately $41,000 per child each year, driven by education costs. Spending drops to around $23,000 for a typical 30-year-old. Older people account for the highest levels of government spending at around $93,000 a year for Australians in their 80s due to health spending, aged care and the Age Pension. Taxation follows

items.title: Did We Solve the Wrong Problem?
items.excerpt: In 2019, Australia fundamentally changed who receives life insurance through superannuation. Two reforms, Protecting Your Super (PYS) and Putting Members’ Interests First (PMIF), were designed to protect retirement balances by reducing unnecessary insurance premiums. The intention was sound. But seven years later, it is worth asking the question. Did we improve retirement outcomes by leaving thousands of Australians uninsured? The rationale behind the reforms was understandable. In superannuation, a dollar deducted at age 20 is not the same as a dollar deducted at age 60. Thanks to the power of compounding, even relatively small deductions early in a member’s working life can have a meaningful impact on their retirement balance decades later. A premium paid today is not just the premium itself. It is the investment returns that premium may otherwise have earned over forty years. That was the concern driving the reforms. Why should a 19-year-old casual worker pay for insurance they may never need? Why should someone with a small super balance watch it slowly erode through premiums for cover they may not even know they have? Viewed solely through the lens of retirement savings, the reforms made sense. But retirement balances are only one measure of member outcomes. Insurance is a pool The case for PMIF and PYS was straightforward. If a member doesn’t need insurance, why should they pay for it? But insurance has never worked that way. Insurance only works because not everyone gets back what they contribute. Most members will never make a claim. A relatively small number will suffer death, disability or serious illness and receive benefits far greater than the premiums they have paid. That isn’t a flaw in the system. That is the system. Insurance works because members collectively protect one another from life’s biggest financial risks. Protecting retirement balances matters. So does protecting the people those balances belong to. Death doesn’t wait until 25 Death, disability and serious illness don’t wait for someone to turn 25 or for their super balance to exceed $6,000. Yet now many younger members no longer receive default insurance unless they actively opt in.

items.title: The Chart That Changed an Industry
items.excerpt: In 2014, ASIC released REP 413, its landmark review of retail life insurance advice. The report would become one of the most influential documents in the history of Australia’s life insurance industry. It triggered industry reviews. It influenced government policy. It shaped public debate. And ultimately it laid the foundations for the Life Insurance Framework (LIF). Nearly a decade later, I have a simple question. Has anyone asked whether it actually worked? How We Got Here To understand whether LIF worked, we first need to acknowledge that it was trying to solve a genuine problem. This wasn’t a case of regulators waking up one morning and deciding to reduce commissions. LIF was the culmination of almost a decade of reform aimed at restoring trust in financial advice. The FOFA Era The Future of Financial Advice (FOFA) reforms fundamentally changed Australia’s approach to financial advice. Following high-profile investment scandals, the focus shifted towards conflicted remuneration. The philosophy was simple: Advice should be driven by client interests, not adviser incentives. Investment commissions were largely removed because policymakers believed embedded remuneration structures created conflicts that distorted advice outcomes. Life insurance, however, was treated differently. There was widespread recognition that life insurance advice wasn’t like investment advice. There was no investment balance from which fees could be deducted. No growing pool of assets to fund advice. The concern was that banning commissions entirely would dramatically reduce access to insurance advice. Life insurance commissions survived FOFA. But only just. REP 413: The Spark In 2014, ASIC released REP 413, reviewing life insurance advice provided between 2006 and 2013. The findings were concerning. ASIC concluded that approximately 37% of advice files reviewed failed to comply with ASIC’s requirements. For a report that ultimately reshaped an entire industry, the evidentiary base was remarkably narrow. More importantly, ASIC observed that advisers receiving higher upfront commissions were more likely to provide advice that ASIC assessed as poor quality. This became the defining narrat

items.title: Nobody Asks the Loss Ratio of a Couch
items.excerpt: Before we go any further, let’s start with a simple question. What is a loss ratio? A loss ratio is simply the amount an insurer pays in claims divided by the premiums it receives. If an insurer collects $100 in premiums and pays $50 in claims, it has a 50% loss ratio. Sounds simple enough. The problem is that many people assume a higher loss ratio automatically means better value. I’m not convinced that’s true. If I told you a life insurance company only paid out around 50 cents in claims for every dollar of premium it received, would you consider that good value? My first reaction was that it sounded poor value. In fact, many people would probably think it sounds terrible. After all, where did the other 50 cents go? Let’s consider the two pillars of Life Insurance in Australia – Retail and Group. In retail advised life insurance, insurers will often target a loss ratio somewhere around 45-50%. In group insurance, via superannuation funds, loss ratios can often target 80%. At first glance, that makes group insurance look like better value. If one product has a loss ratio of 80% and another has a loss ratio of 50%, surely the 80% product is better value? If you’re expecting an actuary to answer this question with a simple yes or no, you’re going to be disappointed. One of our favourite phrases is: “It depends.” I’ve spent much of my career pricing life insurance products, and one thing I’ve learned is that a 50% loss ratio doesn’t necessarily tell you much about profitability, and an 80% loss ratio doesn’t necessarily tell you much about value. I’ve seen products with lower loss ratios generate less profit than products with much higher loss ratios. The economics are far more complicated than many people realise. Why an 80% loss ratio isn’t always better than 50% The difference isn’t because retail insurers are making a fortune. The economics are simply different. In retail insurance, a significant portion of the premium goes towards things that don’t exist in most group arrangements. Advice commissions. Underwriting. Medical evidence. Policy administration (at least more complicated policy administration). Individual servicing. Claims assessment. Retail insuran

items.title: Make Friends With the Reinsurance People
items.excerpt: Early in my career, I noticed that the reinsurance people seemed to have more fun. While the rest of us were in pricing committees, governance forums and assumption reviews, they were off having “market meetings”. Somehow, they had an unusual number of lunches and possessed a remarkable ability to classify golf as an essential component of prudent risk management. If you’re early in your insurance career, my advice is simple. Make friends with the people in reinsurance. They’ll tell you where to get a decent lunch. More importantly, they’ll probably know something important that everyone else has forgotten. What I’ve noticed over the years is that very few people, including many people working in insurance, can adequately explain what reinsurance actually does. One of the stranger things about Australian life insurance is that we only really have a handful of major life insurers. AIA, TAL, Zurich, Acenda and MetLife dominate the direct market. Yet somehow, we support almost every major global reinsurer. Hannover Re. SCOR. Munich Re. RGA. Gen Re. Pacific Life Re. Swiss Re. PartnerRe. (and almost AXA Re) For a country with barely half a dozen meaningful life insurers, it’s quite unique. In most industries, the supporting ecosystem is smaller than the primary market. In Australian life insurance, it can sometimes feel like the opposite is true. It also raises an obvious question. What exactly do all these reinsurers do? Ask most people and they’ll tell you: “Reinsurance is insurance for insurers.” It’s a neat answer. It’s not wrong. It’s just incomplete. It’s a bit like saying NRL is just five tackles and a kick. Technically correct. Practically useless if you’re trying to understand why some clubs win premierships and why the Eels don’t. It tells us almost nothing about why reinsurance exists. Why Australia attracts so many global reinsurers despite a relatively small number of insurers. Why the same mechanism that smooths volatility can sometimes contribute to premium instability. Or why some of the fastest-growing areas of reinsurance today have more to do with capital optimisation than risk transfer. Most importantly, it obscures one of the more uncomfortable t

items.title: Ten Thousand Documents Nobody Reads
items.excerpt: The introduction of Design and Distribution Obligations (DDO) was a response to a genuine problem. The Royal Commission exposed serious misconduct across financial services. ASIC had already identified concerns with products such as consumer credit insurance, direct life insurance and funeral insurance. Consumers had been sold products they did not understand, products they could never realistically benefit from, or products that simply never should have been sold to them in the first place. Trust had been depleted. In response, policymakers sought mechanisms that would force product manufacturers to think more carefully about who their products were designed for, how they were distributed, and what should happen if things started to go wrong. It is difficult to argue with those objectives. In fact, most people in the industry would probably agree with them. The more interesting question, now that the regime has had time to mature, is whether we are achieving those objectives in the most effective way possible. Because somewhere in Australia today, a compliance team is reviewing a Target Market Determination. They are doing exactly what the law requires. They are confirming that distributors are authorised and appropriately trained. They are documenting how eligibility is assessed and ensuring Product Disclosure Statements are provided. They are defining review triggers, setting reporting periods and documenting complaint thresholds. Lawyers will review the document. Compliance teams will review it. Product managers will review it. Governance committees will review it. The document will be approved. And when it is finished, it will look remarkably similar to the one produced by their competitor down the road. This is not a criticism of the people writing these documents. They are following the rules. The question is whether the rules are directing effort to where consumers receive the greatest benefit. What life insurance TMDs actually say If you place the life insurance TMDs of TAL, AIA, Zurich, NobleOak, OnePath, MetLife and ClearView side by side, the similarities are difficult to ignore. The target market is largely the same: adults with debts, dependants or

items.title: The iExtend Question
items.excerpt: What follows is a personal opinion piece. The views are entirely my own, are not those of my employer or any organisation I’m associated with, and shouldn’t be read as financial, legal or actuarial advice. I’m raising questions, not prescribing answers. A life insurance policy can become most valuable at precisely the moment its owner can least afford to keep it. That paradox sits underneath the emergence of iExtend in Australia, and most of the industry is staring at the wrong part of it. The debate so far has fixed on premiums, ownership structures and investor returns. I think the real issue runs deeper. If external investors can reliably extract value from impaired-life policyholders, then that value should arguably have been available to the policyholders themselves all along. That is the debate worth having. Not whether life settlements are “good” or “bad.” Not whether insurers can legally reprice particular cohorts. The real question is whether the traditional life insurance system has quietly failed the customers who become unwell and can no longer afford their cover, and whether the industry’s current defences against players like iExtend are about to stop working entirely. Where Life Settlements Came From The legal foundation is more than a century old. In the 1911 US Supreme Court case Grigsby v. Russell , Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr. held that a life insurance policy was private property that could be sold or assigned like any other asset. A policy could carry transferable economic value independent of the person it originally protected. The modern market, though, was born in the 1980s during the AIDS crisis. Terminally ill policyholders needed cash and sold their policies to third parties for lump sums; the investor kept paying premiums and collected the death benefit when the insured died. Over the following decades this grew into an institutional asset class drawing in hedge funds, pension funds and specialist investors. The US life settlement market today involves hundreds of billions of dollars of face value in force, with annual transaction volumes in the billions. The market favours a recognisable profile: older lives, large sums insured,

items.title: The Australian Annuity Puzzle— Part 3: Why We Don’t Build Them
items.excerpt: 🔥 In Part 1, the system didn’t require annuities. In Part 2, the system struggled to distribute them. Part 3 is the final straw. Even if demand improves… Even if advice improves… Even if retirees genuinely want more certainty… someone still has to manufacture the product. And that’s where the final constraint sits. 🏗️ We Start With the Balance Sheet When retirement income products are designed, the starting point usually isn’t the retiree. It’s the balance sheet. Capital. Volatility. Liquidity. Asset risk. Earnings strain. Only once those constraints are satisfied do we turn to the customer. So the product becomes shaped by what the system can tolerate. Not by what the retiree actually needs. This isn’t irrational behaviour. It’s exactly how the incentives were built. That’s why the market developed unit linked style longevity products. Most balance sheets in Australia didn’t want investment guarantees. ⚖️ We Made Certainty Expensive For years, the capital framework treated investment guarantees as inherently dangerous. If you wanted to offer: income for life protection against market falls inflation-linked certainty predictable retirement outcomes you paid for it. Through: higher capital requirements earnings volatility balance sheet strain asset restrictions. So the more certainty you tried to provide, the more expensive the product became. The response was predictable. Less guarantee. More flexibility. More risk pushed back onto the retiree. We effectively made certainty the most expensive feature in retirement. 🧠 The Real Problem Is How We Think About Time Annuities are long-term liabilities. They pay income over decades. They are not daily liquidity products. They are not subject to bank-run dynamics. They are designed to absorb volatility over time. And yet we’ve often regulated them as if everything needed to be liquidated tomorrow morning. We apply short-term thinking to long-term promises. That creates distortion. 📉 A Useful Reminder During the Global Financial Crisis, many defined benefit schemes saw their funding positions deteriorate sharply. On a strict mark-to-market basis, it looked alarming. Take UniSuper as an example. At one point, assets f
